hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: Your cards — Q, 7. Your opponent raised before the flop, and you decided to call to see how the hand would develop.
Flop — 6, 8, 8. Your opponent bet, putting pressure on you, and you chose to call to stay in the hand.
Turn — 9. Your opponent bet again, this time with a bigger wager, and you still opted to call to see the river.
River — 4. Your opponent fired a large river bet, making you decide how to proceed with caution.

wrongReasons: 4 A plays similarly, but the correct hand pairs a specific board card that 4 A doesn't. Look at which hole card ranks actually appear on the board.
8 10 makes a three of a kind here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
With only one pair, A 3 wouldn't commit chips across multiple streets. This betting line requires a genuinely made hand from the flop onward.
hand.explanation:
Answer: 7 7

Your opponent flopped **Two Pair** and maintained consistent pressure with bets on all three streets, with increasing sizing, indicating a strong hand that needed protection but wasn't a monster. The fact that they didn't change their betting pattern after the turn and river suggests their hand didn't improve beyond **Two Pair**.
